question: Can you test your gut bacteria?
title: Can You Test Your Gut Bacteria? What Microbiome Tests Can (and Can't) Tell You
metaDescription: Microbiome testing is real science, but the consumer market is a wild west. Scotty breaks down what gut bacteria tests actually measure, what the limits are, and what to do instead.
content:
Yes, you can test your gut bacteria. The technology is real, the science behind it is legitimate, and if you've ever been curious about what's actually living in your gut, the option exists. But I want to be honest with y'all about what you're actually getting when you send a stool sample to a consumer testing company — and what you're probably not getting.

The science of microbiome testing is moving incredibly fast. The consumer market has moved even faster than the science. There are a lot of companies selling gut tests right now. Some are genuinely useful. Some are selling you expensive curiosity with clinical-sounding language. Understanding the difference matters if you're going to make real decisions based on the results.

---

## How Gut Bacteria Testing Works

The standard method for analyzing gut microbiota is through a stool sample. Your stool contains bacteria shed from every part of your intestinal tract, and the microbial community in it is a reasonable proxy for what's living in your colon — though research published in PMC has noted that stool doesn't perfectly represent bacteria living in different segments of the gut, particularly the small intestine.

Once your sample reaches the lab, they perform nucleic acid extraction — pulling the DNA out of all the microbial material in your sample. Then they amplify specific gene sequences, typically the 16S ribosomal RNA gene, which is basically a bacterial fingerprint. Every species has a slightly different version of this gene. By sequencing what's there and comparing it to reference databases, the lab can identify which bacterial species are present and in what relative abundance.

This is called 16S rRNA sequencing, and it's the same technology used in academic microbiome research. More advanced labs use whole-genome shotgun sequencing, which sequences everything in the sample — not just the bacterial fingerprint gene — and can provide more detail about bacterial function, not just identity.

A PMC study on microbiome diagnostics described current capabilities as able to identify hundreds of bacterial species and provide relative abundance data reflecting the overall composition of your gut microbiome. The technology is genuinely impressive.

## What Consumer Tests Can Tell You

A good consumer microbiome test can tell you which bacterial phyla, families, genera, and sometimes species are present in your gut and in what relative proportions. It can tell you whether your diversity looks high or low compared to reference populations. Whether certain key commensal species — beneficial resident bacteria — appear to be present or absent. Whether certain known pathogenic or dysbiosis-associated bacteria are elevated.

Some of that information is genuinely useful, especially if you've recently been through antibiotic treatment and want to see the impact. A before-and-after comparison of microbiome composition is something you can actually act on. If certain beneficial species are wiped out, you can specifically target those with fermented foods or targeted probiotic supplementation.

Microbiome diversity is also a meaningful metric. Higher alpha diversity — more different species present — is consistently associated with better health outcomes in the research literature. The NIH's National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences has written extensively on the microbiome-health connection, and diversity consistently appears as a key positive indicator.

## What Consumer Tests Cannot Tell You

Here's where y'all need to pump the brakes a little bit, because the marketing around consumer gut tests often implies more certainty than the science supports.

A 2025 study published in Nature Communications Biology evaluated the analytical performance of direct-to-consumer microbiome testing services and found significant variability in results across different companies testing the same samples. Consistency and reproducibility were not where they'd need to be for clinical decision-making.

More fundamentally: having a particular species present in your stool doesn't tell you what that species is doing. Two people can have the same bacteria in their gut and one is healthy and one is not, because microbiome function depends on the whole community, the diet, the host genetics, and the environment — not on any single species. The research linking specific microbiome compositions to specific health outcomes is still being worked out. The field is moving fast but it's not there yet.

There are no established normal ranges validated across diverse populations. What looks like a deficiency on a consumer test might be perfectly normal for your genetics, your diet, your geography.

## What Actually Builds a Healthy Gut

Here's my practical position. You can spend a couple hundred dollars on a gut test, or you can spend that money on real fermented vegetables and raw foods and feed your gut directly. I know which one I'd do.

The best way to build a healthy gut microbiome is to expose your gut bacteria to healthy bacteria from the world around you, consistently, over time. Raw vegetables carry bacteria on their surfaces. Fermented vegetables are colonized by dense communities of beneficial lactic acid bacteria. These foods are the original probiotics — not patented, not packaged, not extracted and freeze-dried. Just alive.

Dietary diversity matters enormously. Research consistently shows that people who eat more than 30 different plant species per week have significantly higher gut microbiome diversity than those eating fewer varieties. That's 30 different fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, seeds, herbs, and spices — different types, counted separately. You don't need a test to start working toward that number.

Fiber is the primary food source for your gut bacteria — not the bacteria you eat in supplements, but the bacteria already living in your colon. Without fiber, those populations shrink. With regular diverse fiber intake, they thrive.

If you do want to test — and I'm not against it — use it as a tracking tool rather than a diagnosis. Test, change your diet toward more fermented foods and plant diversity, test again in three months. Look for movement. That's how you get actionable information out of the technology.

But the foundation is food. It has always been food. Test your gut if you're curious. Then feed it right.

---

3. Nature Communications Biology — Evaluating the analytical performance of direct-to-consumer gut microbiome testing services: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-025-09301-3
4. NIH / NIEHS — Microbiome: https://www.niehs.nih.gov/health/topics/science/microbiome
5. PMC / NIH — The gut microbiome — Does stool represent right?: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10123208/
6. PMC / NIH — Fermented Foods, Health and the Gut Microbiome: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9003261/

scottyQuotes: How do we get healthy guts? You get a healthy gut by exposing your bacteria to healthy bacteria out in the world.
The easiest way to do this is through your foods — living raw vegetables and fermented foods.
Beneficial bacteria has a better chance of getting through your stomach than patented probiotic strains.
